metadata.summary: Thin views plus service layer plus drf-spectacular OpenAPI plus ViewSet pattern for Django REST Framework. Views validate input and call services; services own business logic and accept domain types, not request.user.

content.title: Django REST Framework API
content.summary: Thin views plus service layer plus `drf-spectacular` OpenAPI plus `ViewSet` pattern for Django REST Framework. Views validate input and call services; services own all business logic and accept domain types (not `request.user`). Every endpoint gets `@extend_schema` with `summary`, `request`, `responses`, and `tags`. Fat views (business logic in `def post`) are untestable from Celery or management commands and resist refactoring. Separating concerns into serializer → thin view → service → repository lets each layer be tested independently and reused across callers. `drf-spectacular` generates accurate OpenAPI from code; `manage.py spectacular --validate` catches schema regressions before deployment.

why.p: Fat views (business logic in `def post`) are untestable from Celery or management commands and resist refactoring. Separating concerns into serializer → thin view → service → repository lets each layer be tested independently and reused across callers. `drf-spectacular` generates accurate OpenAPI from code; `manage.py spectacular --validate` catches schema regressions before deployment.

list.item: New DRF endpoint inside a Django 5 project following apps/(name)/{views,services,serializers,urls}.py
Refactoring a fat view (business logic in def post) into thin view plus services.py extraction
Adding OpenAPI docs to existing endpoints via `drf-spectacular`
Standardizing pagination, permissions, filtering across a `ViewSet`
FastAPI, Starlette, Flask — different idioms; see python-fastapi instead
Internal-only endpoints used by Django admin or management commands — plain Django views suffice
Pure GraphQL APIs — `graphene-django` or `strawberry-django` have orthogonal patterns
Async-first endpoints with heavy I/O concurrency — use Django Ninja or FastAPI
One-off webhooks needing raw JSON in and out — DRF serializer overhead not justified

rule.statement: Views handle exactly three concerns: (1) validate request input via serializer, (2) call a service function, (3) return the response. All business logic lives in services.
rule.rationale: Fat views (business logic in def post) are untestable from Celery or management commands. Thin views are unit-testable; services are independently testable and reusable.

rule.statement: Services must accept domain types (user_id: int or User model), never request.user directly. This allows calling the same service from Celery, management commands, and tests.
rule.rationale: request.user is an HTTP concern. Service functions called from Celery tasks have no request object. Passing request.user to services couples them to the HTTP layer.

example.title: APIView with Thin View Pattern
example.code:
from rest_framework.views import APIView from rest_framework.response import Response from rest_framework import status from drf_spectacular.utils import extend_schema
from apps.users import services as user_services from . import serializers
class ItemActivationView(APIView): @extend_schema( summary="Activate item", description="Activates item for authenticated user", request=serializers.ItemActivationRequest, responses={200: serializers.ItemActivationResponse}, tags=["Items"], ) def post(self, request): # 1. Validate input serializer = serializers.ItemActivationRequest(data=request.data) serializer.is_valid(raise_exception=True)
# 2. Call service (pass domain type, not request.user) item = user_services.activate_user_item( user_id=request.user.id, item_code=serializer.validated_data["item_code"], )
# 3. Return response return Response( serializers.ItemActivationResponse(item).data, status=status.HTTP_200_OK, )

example.title: Fat View Anti-Pattern

rule.statement: Always scope get_queryset to the current user's data. Never expose Model.objects.all() directly in a ViewSet. This guarantees an IDOR sooner or later.
rule.rationale: An unscoped queryset allows any authenticated user to access any object by ID. Scoping in get_queryset applies to all ViewSet actions automatically.

rule.statement: Whitelist allowed HTTP methods on ModelViewSet via http_method_names. ModelViewSet exposes DELETE by default. Unless deletion is explicitly required, restrict allowed methods to prevent accidental data loss endpoints.
rule.rationale: The default ModelViewSet routes include DELETE. Unless deletion is explicitly required, agents copy-paste and accidentally ship destructive endpoints.

example.title: ModelViewSet with Scoped Queryset

rule.statement: Define separate serializer classes for request input and response output. Never use one ModelSerializer for both. This leaks write-only fields (e.g., password) into responses.
rule.rationale: A combined serializer requires field exclusions or conditional read_only logic per action. Separate classes are simpler, independently testable, and OpenAPI-friendly.

example.title: Separate Request and Response Serializers
Custom Exception Classes
example.code:
from rest_framework.views import exception_handler from rest_framework.exceptions import APIException
class OrderLimitExceeded(APIException): status_code = 400 default_detail = "Daily order limit exceeded" default_code = "order_limit_exceeded"

antipattern.reason: Services called from Celery tasks or management commands have no request object. Pass user_id or the User model instance instead.

antipattern.description: N+1 queries in get_queryset without select_related or prefetch_related.
antipattern.reason: ViewSet.list() iterates the queryset. Without prefetch, each serialized related object issues an additional query. Use django-debug-toolbar to catch this in development.

antipattern.description: Using force_authenticate(user=user) in all tests without any unauthenticated tests.
antipattern.reason: force_authenticate bypasses permission classes. Tests stay green even when permissions are misconfigured. Always include at least one test asserting 401 or 403 for each endpoint.

antipattern.description: Treating PUT as equivalent to PATCH and sending partial updates to PUT endpoints.
antipattern.reason: ModelViewSet.update defaults to PUT (full replace). Sending partial data results in unset fields being nulled. Use PATCH for partial updates and patch() in tests.

antipattern.description: Missing tags= in @extend_schema. All endpoints grouped under "default" in Swagger UI.
antipattern.reason: Without tags, Swagger UI lists every endpoint in one undifferentiated group, making the docs unusable for APIs with more than 10 endpoints.

rule.statement: Run manage.py spectacular --validate before merging. This catches broken OpenAPI schemas that would silently produce incomplete documentation.
rule.rationale: drf-spectacular infers schemas from serializers. Certain field combinations (nullable plus optional) produce incorrect OpenAPI without runtime errors. Validation catches them early.

template.purpose: CI script: export and diff OpenAPI schema, fail on breaking changes
Prompt to scaffold serializer plus service plus view plus tests atomically
